summary: Joel Spolsky posts a casual, multi-topic entry covering three unrelated subjects: choosing a DSL provider in New York, designing a reliable web service architecture with geographically distributed SQL Server backups, and recommending a monospaced programming font. On DSL, he's frustrated by the blame game between providers and Verizon and wants to simplify by going direct. On reliability, he outlines a hot-backup scheme using transaction log shipping between servers on opposite coasts. He preemptively deflects any Linux suggestions, admitting he lacks the skills to maintain Linux but can patch Windows easily.
key_points: Joel prefers paying a 'monopoly tax' to Verizon directly rather than dealing with blame-shifting between DSL resellers and the underlying provider
He outlines a geographic redundancy scheme using SQL Server transaction log shipping between New York and Vancouver for failover
He makes a pragmatic argument for Windows over Linux: security depends on the operator's skills, not the OS's theoretical superiority
He recommends ProFont as an ideal tiny monospaced font for programming, noting that even with larger monitors, small fonts remain necessary to see enough code
money_quotes: I'd be willing to pay the monopoly tax if it meant when our DSL went down there was nobody left to blame.
Last time a flaw was discovered in Windows, it took me two clicks to patch it. Last time a flaw was discovered in SSH, it took me four hours of compiling and messing around to patch it.
I apologize but I don't have the skilz to keep a Linux box secure.
Now that I have two 18″ LCD panels, the only way to see a reasonable amount of code on screen is to use a tiny font.
The world is awash in lovely TrueType fonts but none of them are monospaced, which is a nuisance for programming because things which should line up won't.
key_insight: Practical reliability depends on the operator's actual skills and the simplicity of the system, not on theoretically superior technology.
